upperAir: The KOAK sounding shows a notably stable lower atmosphere with a surface-to-850mb lapse rate of only -0.53°C/km (far below dry adiabatic 9.8°C/km), indicating a strong low-level inversion that will suppress thermals through mid-morning. The 850mb temp is 13.2°C — warm aloft — capping convection and limiting thermal ceiling to roughly 1500m MSL at best. Wind shear is present: surface winds light and variable, 300°/15 kts at 850mb, veering to 305°/15 kts at 700mb, then backing to 245°/25 kts at 500mb — indicating classic afternoon NW sea breeze reinforcement below with upper-level westerlies above.
aviationFlags: No active AIRMETs for the Bay Area. KSFO reporting 290-300° at 14-17 kts with BKN150 by 2156Z, suggesting afternoon marine push. KHAF reporting 320-330° at 10-13 kts CLR — coastal flow active and clean. KRHV at 320°/10 kts with FEW050 by 2147Z — inland NW flow established, confirming afternoon sea breeze arrival at Ed Levin/Mission Peak. KAPC 200°/9 kts, 24°C — Napa seeing thermal/valley southerly, slight gradient. KWVI 230°/8-11 kts — south bay marine influence consistent.

sites.name: Ed Levin (1750 ft)
sites.howItWorks: Ed Levin high launch is a thermal and ridge hybrid site. It needs NW to W winds at 8-15 kts for good ridge soaring; thermals develop inland from late morning once the stable layer breaks. Sea breeze front typically arrives from the NW by early-to-mid afternoon, enhancing lift but also increasing wind speeds.
sites.todaySetup: Today's key story at Ed Levin high launch is a slow thermal start due to the strong low-level inversion, followed by a solid NW sea breeze window in the early-to-mid afternoon. The sounding's stable lapse rate (-0.53°C/km) means thermals will be weak and broken until at least 11 AM–noon. The BL rises gradually from ~125m at 8 AM to 420-455m by 1-3 PM, with CAPE spiking to 180 J/kg at 1 PM — enough for usable thermals but not overdeveloped. Wind direction is consistently NW (296-318°) through the afternoon, ideal for the launch. HRRR and GFS agree: winds build from ~1-5 kts in the morning to 8-10 kts by 2-4 PM. KRHV reports 32010KT confirming the NW sea breeze has arrived inland. Late afternoon (4-5 PM) winds push 10-11 kts at 80m altitude with 93-100% cloud cover rolling in at 4 PM — likely a marine push overrunning thermals. The window is real but relatively narrow: 12:30–3:30 PM.

hourlyWindows.label: Morning (8–11 AM)
hourlyWindows.summary: Wind 1.5-3 kts and variable (NW-W), BL only 125-360m — thermals barely firing under a strong inversion. Not launchable in useful conditions; too light and air too stable.

hourlyWindows.label: Midday (11 AM–2 PM)
hourlyWindows.summary: Winds build to 5-8 kts NW by noon, BL reaches 410-420m with CAPE 60-180 J/kg — thermals developing and ridge lift coming in. Best window opening around 12:30 PM; conditions tightening and workable for P2+.

hourlyWindows.label: Afternoon (2 PM–sunset)
hourlyWindows.summary: Winds 8-10 kts NW at 2-3 PM is the sweet spot; by 4 PM winds hit 10-11 kts at surface with 80m-level winds at 12 kts and 93-100% cloud cover shutting down thermals. Marine push likely overruns the site by 4-5 PM — plan to land before then.

verdict.hazards: Late marine push by 4 PM with rapidly increasing winds and cloud; strong inversion suppresses thermals all morning; wind can cycle up quickly

sites.name: Ed Levin (600 ft)
sites.howItWorks: The 600 ft launch is the lower training hill at Ed Levin, primarily a thermal site with a shorter glide to the LZ. It requires lighter, more stable conditions than the high launch and is preferred for P2s practicing thermalling in moderate conditions. Needs NW winds 6-12 kts and an established BL.
sites.todaySetup: The 600 ft launch faces the same inversion problem as the high launch — the BL is only 115-205m through 9 AM, making early thermals nonexistent. However, conditions align by midday: winds build to 6-8 kts NW by noon (296-306°), BL reaches 415-485m by 1-3 PM, which is a reasonable thermal ceiling for a lower launch. CAPE of 190 J/kg at 1 PM suggests moderate thermals. The afternoon progression mirrors the high launch — winds reach 9-11 kts by 3-4 PM, then the marine push drives surface winds to 11-12 kts NW-NNW (314-331°) and 80m winds to 13-14 kts by 4-7 PM — on the strong side for a lower training launch and potentially gusty/rowdy near terrain. KRHV's 32010KT confirms the onshore push is real. Good window exists for competent P2s in the 12:30–3 PM slot.

hourlyWindows.label: Morning (8–11 AM)
hourlyWindows.summary: Wind 3.5-4.5 kts variable (251-330°), BL under 360m — thermals not established, too light and inconsistent. Unsuitable for flight.

hourlyWindows.label: Midday (11 AM–2 PM)
hourlyWindows.summary: Winds steadying NW at 6-8 kts by noon, BL 415-455m with CAPE building — thermals usable. Window opens around 12:30 PM; solid P2 conditions with manageable lift.

hourlyWindows.label: Afternoon (2 PM–sunset)
hourlyWindows.summary: Best lift 2-3 PM with 8-10 kts NW; by 4 PM winds jump to 11 kts surface / 13 kts at 80m as marine push arrives — too strong and gusty for lower launch. Be down by 3:30 PM.

verdict.hazards: Strong marine push by 4 PM making conditions too powerful for lower launch; morning too stable; afternoon winds punchy above 3 PM

sites.name: Mussel Rock
sites.howItWorks: Mussel Rock is a pure ridge-soaring site that lives on consistent W to NW sea breeze at 8-20 kts. The cliff faces the open Pacific, generating strong mechanical lift when marine winds are on. Marine layer presence is critical — too thick and it sits in the clouds; too thin and it burns off leaving light winds. Ideal: 10-18 kts W-NW, marine layer below launch height.
sites.todaySetup: Today at Mussel Rock is a marginal but potentially flyable afternoon window, contingent on marine layer burn-off. The site is socked in all morning (100% cloud cover through 11 AM per both HRRR and GFS) with winds light at 2-7 kts — unlaunchable. The marine layer shows signs of thinning by 1-2 PM (cloud drops to 3-21% at 1-2 PM per HRRR), allowing ridge lift to develop as winds build to 7-9 kts W-NW (274-285°) — right on the ideal window for this site. SST at 14.2°C (both SF Bar and Half Moon Bay buoys) is cool but not exceptional, and the 13-second wave period suggests a moderate marine layer that could hold longer than models suggest. KHAF is reporting 320°/11-13 kts CLR at 16°C — marine air is flowing but the coastal ridge appears clear south of the site. The 3-6 PM window looks best with 8-10 kts at surface and 10-12 kts at 80m, direction holding W to WNW. Watch for the afternoon marine push re-clouding the site by 5-6 PM.

hourlyWindows.label: Morning (8–11 AM)
hourlyWindows.summary: 100% cloud cover, winds 2-7 kts W — marine layer firmly in place. Site is socked in; no flight possible.

hourlyWindows.label: Midday (11 AM–2 PM)
hourlyWindows.summary: Marine layer begins to thin by 1 PM (clouds 3-21%), winds 6-8 kts W-NW — transitional period. Launch is conditional on actual visual clearing; scout before committing.

hourlyWindows.label: Afternoon (2 PM–sunset)
hourlyWindows.summary: Best window 2-5 PM: winds 7-10 kts W-NW, cloud 6-33%, solid ridge lift possible. By 5-6 PM clouds rebuild to 82-100% and winds hold at 9 kts — soarable but visibility may deteriorate.

verdict.hazards: Marine layer holding until early afternoon — confirm visual clearing before launch; re-clouding by 5-6 PM; cool SST (14.2°C) and 13s wave period suggest layer may be stubborn; light morning winds offer no buffer

sites.name: Mission Peak
sites.locationDescriptor: Fremont — inland ridge/thermal site, W-NW facing
sites.howItWorks: Mission Peak combines ridge lift with strong thermals when the inland valley heats up. It needs NW to W winds 8-15 kts at ridge height and a solid BL for thermals to work. The sea breeze front arrival from the bay typically enhances afternoon conditions but can also bring gusty, punchy air if it comes in strong.
sites.todaySetup: Mission Peak tells essentially the same story as Ed Levin — same stable inversion suppressing morning thermals, same NW sea breeze building in the afternoon. The BL is only 125-215m through 9 AM, thermal triggering delayed until 11-12 PM. By 1-3 PM, the BL reaches 420-455m with CAPE of 60-180 J/kg — adequate for moderate thermals. Winds build from near-calm in the morning to 7-9 kts NW by 1-3 PM (296-318°), consistent with KRHV's observed 32010KT. The afternoon window from about 12:30-3:30 PM is legitimate, with the marine push arriving by 4 PM driving winds to 10-11 kts and cloud cover to 93-100%. The higher altitude here (compared to 600 ft launch at Ed Levin) and more exposed ridge means the punchy sea breeze cycles will be felt more acutely — expect thermal/mechanical mixing as conditions ramp up. P3 experience recommended given the afternoon timing and sea breeze characteristics at this site.

hourlyWindows.label: Morning (8–11 AM)
hourlyWindows.summary: Winds 1.4-3.3 kts variable, BL under 360m — stable inversion capping thermals. No viable lift; site effectively dead until late morning.

hourlyWindows.label: Midday (11 AM–2 PM)
hourlyWindows.summary: Winds build to 5-8 kts NW by noon, thermals beginning to fire with BL at 410-420m and CAPE 60-180 J/kg. Window opening around 12:30 PM; best for P3 pilots comfortable with thermal/mechanical mix.

hourlyWindows.label: Afternoon (2 PM–sunset)
hourlyWindows.summary: Peak window 2-3:30 PM with 8-9 kts NW; by 4 PM the marine push brings 10+ kts, high cloud, and increasing rotor/turbulence risk on lee side. Land before 4 PM.

verdict.hazards: Morning inversion kills early thermals; marine push by 4 PM brings gusty 10+ kts and full cloud cover; thermal/mechanical turbulence during sea breeze arrival

tomorrow.name: Ed Levin (1750 ft)
tomorrow.analysis: Thursday April 9 looks problematic — 100% cloud cover all day per both HRRR and GFS, with BL starting at only 120m. Winds shift to SW-WSW (236-297°) at 6-11 kts. Tomorrow.io shows only 5-6% cloud in the AM but rapidly overcast by noon, with visibility dropping to 5 km at 2 PM — likely marine layer intrusion. CAPE builds to 210-260 J/kg by 2-4 PM but the overcast will suppress thermal activation. Marginal at best; cloud cover is the main killer.

tomorrow.name: Ed Levin (600 ft)
tomorrow.analysis: Similar story to high launch — full overcast by midday with SSW flow at 5-7 kts. Tomorrow.io keeps the morning partly clear (19-29% cloud) with light NNW winds, but afternoon goes 100% overcast. Not a great day; possibly early morning glass-off if marine layer is thin at the lower site, but the trend is poor.

tomorrow.name: Mussel Rock
tomorrow.analysis: Thursday looks like a southerly wind day at Mussel Rock — GFS/HRRR both show 208-244° flow at 5-13 kts all day, building to 12-17 kts by late afternoon. South/SSW winds are cross-shore or slightly offshore at this W-NW facing site, meaning no ridge lift. 100% cloud cover all day. Tomorrow.io confirms light W winds early then transition to S. Likely NO FLY.

tomorrow.name: Mission Peak
tomorrow.analysis: Full overcast all day Thursday, same as Ed Levin. Winds SW-WSW at 6-11 kts, BL reaching 770m with 210 J/kg CAPE by 2 PM — thermals trying to fire but cloud deck suppresses them. Tomorrow.io visibility drops to near zero at Mission Peak at 8 AM (0.0 km), clearing slightly later. A socked-in, marginal day; only a small afternoon window possible if cloud breaks.

day2.name: Ed Levin (1750 ft)
day2.analysis: Friday April 10 brings the most active setup of the week — CAPE spikes to 350-360 J/kg at noon, BL climbs to 1435-1825m, and precipitation probability at 53-71%. This is a convective/unstable day with strong S-SW surface winds (193-220°) at 5-9 kts building to 12 kts at 80m by afternoon. Strong thermals but high overdevelopment risk, possible showers. Not a safe paragliding day — the combination of high CAPE, high precip probability, and strong wind shear makes this a NO FLY.

day2.name: Ed Levin (600 ft)
day2.analysis: Same convective concern as high launch on Friday — CAPE 350 J/kg, BL 1435m, 67% precip probability by noon. S-SW winds at surface. Strong but dangerous thermal day with shower risk. NO FLY.

day2.name: Mussel Rock
day2.analysis: Friday at Mussel Rock: winds SSW at 9-11 kts, building to 11-14 kts at 80m. CAPE 220-320 J/kg, 62% precip probability, BL 1000-1290m. South component winds are poor for ridge soaring here. Active, unstable atmosphere. NO FLY.

day2.name: Mission Peak
day2.analysis: Friday is the most thermally aggressive day of the forecast — BL to 1855m, CAPE 330-440 J/kg, but 69% precip probability. SSW winds at surface 5-9 kts, strong at altitude. Classic over-development risk day. Avoid flying — high chance of showers and unstable, unpredictable thermals. NO FLY.

day3.name: Ed Levin (1750 ft)
day3.analysis: Day 3 (Saturday April 11) data not yet in the 4-day window shown; GFS data ends at April 10. Monitor updated forecasts for Saturday — the Friday convective system will need to track and determine if it clears by Saturday morning.

day3.name: Ed Levin (600 ft)
day3.analysis: Same as high launch — Saturday data beyond current model window. Watch for post-frontal clearing from the Friday convective episode; could reset to better NW flow if the system moves through quickly.

day3.name: Mussel Rock
day3.analysis: Saturday outlook beyond current model window. If Friday's instability clears, post-system NW flow could bring good ridge conditions — but timing depends on how fast the Friday pattern flushes through. Check updated models.

day3.name: Mission Peak
day3.analysis: Saturday beyond current data. Post-frontal NW flow after Friday's convective day is possible; thermal quality could improve significantly if the system clears. Monitor closely.

watchlist: Marine layer burn-off timing at Mussel Rock is the critical uncertainty today — cool SST (14.2°C) and 13-second wave period at Half Moon Bay buoy suggest the layer could be stubborn past noon; verify on-site before committing
KRHV 32010KT confirms the afternoon NW sea breeze has arrived inland — Ed Levin/Mission Peak pilots should plan to be down by 3:30 PM before marine push intensifies
Strong low-level inversion confirmed by KOAK sounding (only -0.53°C/km lapse rate) will kill thermals at all inland sites until at least 11:30 AM–noon; do not launch early expecting to climb
Thursday (April 9): full overcast at all sites all day — a rest day is likely
Friday (April 10): high CAPE (350+ J/kg), 60-70%+ precip probability, and strong wind shear — convective/shower day, all sites NO FLY; plan accordingly for weekend
tldr: Today's best bet is Ed Levin 1750 ft or Mission Peak in the 12:30–3:30 PM window — the NW sea breeze fills in nicely at 8-10 kts once the morning inversion breaks. Mussel Rock is possible but needs you to physically verify marine layer burn-off before you unpack. Thursday and Friday look poor (overcast then convective), so today is the window of the week — don't sleep on it.
